source: Wink: Recovering from Misbehaviors in Coding Agents
summary: Lightweight asynchronous self-intervention system for coding agents that detects specification drift, reasoning loops, and tool call failures (~30% of production trajectories) and injects targeted course-correction prompts without killing the agent process.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — directly addresses AgentForge's lack of mid-run recovery; ProcessRunner already emits stdout chunks via RunEventEmitter making a subscriber-based monitor feasible

summary: Northwestern University study showing frontier LLM agents are systematically over-optimistic on doomed trajectories; early stopping saves 28–64% tokens on failed runs; budget-awareness is trainable via SFT+RL.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — AgentForge has no mid-run tool-call accounting; adding a BudgetTracker to ProcessRunner with early abort requires no model changes and addresses the 'no test-time compute scaling' limitation

source: Budget-Aware Tool-Use Enables Effective Agent Scaling
summary: Demonstrates that granting agents a larger tool-call budget alone fails to improve performance; introducing a lightweight Budget Tracker plug-in providing continuous budget awareness enables effective scaling.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — complementary to BAGEN; provides the specific plug-in architecture pattern used for HID-100 design

source: Speculative Actions: A Lossless Framework for Faster Agentic Systems
summary: Columbia University paper showing that using faster models to speculatively pre-execute likely next actions during idle windows achieves up to 20% latency reduction with ~55% prediction accuracy across gaming, e-commerce, and web search environments.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — AgentForge has a natural idle window during PlanReview (Claude idle, Codex active); adapted as speculative skeleton generation rather than full speculative execution

source: SelfBudgeter: Adaptive Token Allocation for Efficient LLM Reasoning
summary: Peking University framework for pre-estimating token budget based on problem difficulty before reasoning; achieves 61–74% response length compression while maintaining accuracy via dual-phase SFT+RL training.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — focuses on model-level training rather than orchestration-level budgeting; the pre-estimation aspect is similar to HID-79 (complexity-aware token budget estimation) already planned

summary: Agents provided with workflow snippets from successful prior runs on similar tasks improve 8.9–14.0 absolute points on benchmarks; workflow memory generalizes well within similar task types.
relevanceToAgentForge: High in principle, but HID-92 (cross-run heuristic memory via experiential reflective learning) already covers this pattern; would duplicate existing planned work

source: OpenTelemetry for LLMs / LLM Observability 2026 Survey
summary: Production LLM observability pattern using OTel spans for per-stage cost, latency, and quality tracking; tools like Langfuse, Arize AI, and Helicone provide per-feature/per-team visibility.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — HID-52, HID-83, HID-58 already cover per-stage execution telemetry and cost-tracking API; this does not add novel architectural patterns beyond those

source: Mem0 State of AI Agent Memory 2026
summary: Comprehensive benchmarks of agent memory architectures in 2026; identifies cross-session learning from execution traces as highest-value unsolved production gap; highlights that 1M-token contexts are making simple vector stores cost-competitive.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — useful for future memory architecture decisions; current memory gaps already addressed by HID-27, HID-84, HID-86, HID-92

source: Anytime Verified Agents (AVA) — Adaptive Compute Allocation
summary: Framework for dynamically allocating compute across search, sampling, and verification within a user-specified budget; uses calibrated uncertainty estimation and value-of-information-guided search expansion.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — interesting verification scaling concept but requires model-level uncertainty estimates not available from CLI subprocess agents; feasibility too low for current architecture

source: OpenClaw / trending CLI coding agent repos 2026
summary: OpenClaw (210k+ stars), Claw Code (fastest repo to 100k stars) — clean-room rewrites of Claude Code architecture in Python/Rust; Langflow/Dify/n8n for visual pipeline design; all represent CLI agent runners rather than orchestration innovations.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — these are alternative CLI agent runtimes, not orchestration patterns applicable to AgentForge's state-machine model; no novel orchestration insights

summary: Prioritizes tool-call budget over token budget as the more direct constraint on agent capability; demonstrates that tool-call limits are more actionable than token limits for agentic scaling.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — core architectural insight used in HID-100 design (tool-call counting vs token counting); already included via HID-100
